---
title: "Strahler order is a coarsening of the flow-accumulation merge tree"
subtitle: "Phase A proof skeleton — statements and obligations filled, arguments TO DRAFT"
author: "Michael Kerr"
date: "2026-05-26"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    html-math-method: katex
---

> **Skeleton status.** This is a Phase A *skeleton*, not a draft proof.
> Definitions in §1 are starting points to revise. Theorem and lemma
> *statements* are the claims converged on over four rounds of plan
> review. Every *argument* is a `[TO DRAFT — MK]` obligation stub:
> the act of writing the proof is the verification step (three of the
> four bugs caught in planning were prose-survivable errors that died
> only when the construction was traced concretely), so the arguments
> are deliberately left for hand-derivation. The falsification witness
> in §4 is given as the corrected equal-birth construction so it is
> not re-derived from the stale round-3 "permute children" version.
>
> Claude's role on the filled draft: notation/LaTeX normalization, a
> referee pass ("find the weakest claim and show where the proof
> depends on it"), and a targeted check that §4's argument matches the
> equal-birth construction below.

# 1. Setup

Definitions are starting points; revise notation to taste.

**Grid and elevation.** Let $G$ be a finite set of grid cells with the
usual 8-neighbour spatial adjacency. Let $z : G \to \mathbb{R}$ be the
raw DEM elevation.

**Hydrologic conditioning.** Let $z' : G \to \mathbb{R}$ be the
elevation after whitebox's breach-and-fill conditioning. Every
construction below operates on $z'$, not $z$; this is stated here in
the setup, not deferred to a footnote, because the filtration is an
object of the *modified* field.

**D8 routing.** Let $r : G \to G \cup \{\bot\}$ be the D8
single-receiver function: $r(c)$ is the steepest-descent 8-neighbour
of $c$ under $z'$, with $r(c) = \bot$ at outlets and grid edges. D8 is
a *scope condition*, not an incidental choice (see §6).

**Donor relation.** $\operatorname{don}(c) = \{\, x \in G : r(x) = c \,\}$.
Because $r$ is a function, the directed graph $\{x \to r(x)\}$ is a
forest of in-trees rooted at outlets; this fact is load-bearing for
§5's stability scope and §6's multi-outlet case.

**Flow accumulation.** $A(c) = \bigl|\{\, x \in G : x \leadsto c \,\}\bigr|$,
where $\leadsto$ is the reflexive-transitive closure of $r$.
Equivalently $A(c) = 1 + \sum_{x \in \operatorname{don}(c)} A(x)$.
Accumulation increases strictly downstream: $r(c) \neq \bot \implies
A(r(c)) > A(c)$.

**Channel mask.** Fix a channelization threshold
$\tau_{\mathrm{chan}}$. The channel mask is
$M = \{\, c \in G : A(c) \ge \tau_{\mathrm{chan}} \,\}$.
$\tau_{\mathrm{chan}}$ is a modeling parameter, not a free variable of
any theorem below (see §6).

**Donor graph on the mask.** Let $\mathcal{G} = (M, E)$ be the
*undirected* graph whose edges are $\{c, r(c)\}$ for $c \in M$ with
$r(c) \in M$. This is the donor-graph adjacency. It is **not** the
spatial 8-adjacency restricted to $M$; the two coincide on toys
without subparallel tributaries or meander necks but diverge on real
terrain (Phase 0 spike,
[`../notes/orientation_spike.md`](../notes/orientation_spike.md)).

**Merge tree under donor adjacency.** The merge tree $T$ of
$(M, A, \mathcal{G})$ tracks connected components of the sublevel sets
$M_{\le t} = \{\, c \in M : A(c) \le t \,\}$ as $t$ increases, where
connectivity uses $\mathcal{G}$-edges only. A component is *born* at a
cell with no in-mask donors (a channel head, a local minimum of
$A|_M$ in $\mathcal{G}$) and components *merge* at a cell with
$\ge 2$ in-mask donor components (a confluence). The persistence
diagram $\operatorname{Dgm}_0(A|_M)$ is the multiset of
$(\text{birth}, \text{death})$ pairs of $T$.

# 2. Lemma (bijection: donor-graph merge tree $\cong$ channel network)

**Lemma.** The merge tree $T$ of $(M, A, \mathcal{G})$, as a rooted
tree with each edge labeled by its $A$-interval, is isomorphic to the
channel network extracted by the standard hydrology workflow
(threshold $A$ at $\tau_{\mathrm{chan}}$; extract maximal channel
segments between junctions; root at the outlet). Under the
isomorphism: leaves $\leftrightarrow$ channel heads, internal nodes
$\leftrightarrow$ confluences, root $\leftrightarrow$ outlet.

*Remark (positioning, per [`../notes/folklore_check.md`](../notes/folklore_check.md)).*
This lemma formalizes a folklore correspondence approached from three
sides by prior work (TerraStream's elevation-persistence + Pfafstetter
pipeline; Cousty et al. watershed-as-MSF; Horton-Strahler tree-pruning
combinatorics) without uniting them on the flow-accumulation scalar.
State it as a clarifying lemma, not a headline result; the novelty is
the *donor-graph adjacency* (no prior terrain merge tree is built on
the flow graph rather than spatial adjacency) and the downstream
contributions in §3-§4 and the contrast in §7.

*Proof obligation.* `[TO DRAFT — MK]` Show the bijection by tracing the
ascending-$A$ sweep cell-by-cell: (i) a cell with no in-mask donor is
exactly a channel head; (ii) a cell with one in-mask donor component
extends a channel segment; (iii) a cell with $\ge 2$ in-mask donor
components is exactly a junction; (iv) the strictly-increasing
property of $A$ downstream guarantees each cell is processed after all
its donors, so the sweep is well-defined. Walk the branching-channel
toy concretely (as the Y-toy was walked) to confirm the
correspondence before generalizing.

# 3. Theorem 1 (Strahler order is a coarsening of $T$)

**Definition (Strahler map on a rooted tree).** $S : T \to
\mathbb{Z}_{>0}$, bottom-up: $S(\text{leaf}) = 1$; for an internal
node with children of orders $o_1 \ge o_2 \ge \dots$, set
$S = o_1 + 1$ if $o_1 = o_2$, else $S = o_1$.

**Theorem 1.** The Horton-Strahler stream order of the channel network
(via the Lemma's isomorphism) equals $S$ applied to $T$. Consequently,
the standard hydrology summaries — Horton bifurcation ratio $R_b$,
length ratio $R_l$, and the Hack exponent computed from channel
lengths — factor through $T$: each is a function of $T$ alone, hence a
coarsening of the merge tree.

*Proof obligation.* `[TO DRAFT — MK]` (a) The Strahler equality is
immediate from the Lemma plus the definition of $S$, but state the
correspondence of confluence-types (equal-order vs unequal-order
junctions) explicitly. (b) For the "factor through $T$" claim, exhibit
each summary as a function of the tree's combinatorial type and edge
labels; this is what makes "coarsening" precise and is the sentence a
hydrologist referee will check.

# 4. Theorem 2 (the persistence diagram is a lossy coarsening of $T$)

**Theorem 2.** $\operatorname{Dgm}_0(A|_M)$ is the abelianization of
$T$: it records the multiset of $(\text{birth}, \text{death})$
intervals but discards the branching pairing (which child merges into
which parent). There exist DEMs $M_1, M_2$ with
$d_B\bigl(\operatorname{Dgm}_0(A|_{M_1}),
\operatorname{Dgm}_0(A|_{M_2})\bigr) = 0$ but distinct Strahler
distributions. Hence Strahler order (Theorem 1) cannot factor through
$\operatorname{Dgm}_0$; the merge tree is the right invariant.

**Falsification witness — equal-birth construction (given).** Use four
channel heads, all entering at $A = \tau_{\mathrm{chan}}$, so births
are degenerate at a common value $\tau$. Then the barcode is
determined by the death (confluence) heights alone, independent of the
branching:

- *Tree A (balanced).* Two head-pairs merge at confluence heights
  $h_1, h_2$; the two resulting order-2 streams merge at $h_3$.
  Confluence orders $\{2, 2, 3\}$, root order $3$.
- *Tree B (caterpillar).* Heads join a single main stem successively
  at $h_1, h_2, h_3$; each junction is order-2-meets-order-1, so the
  output stays order $2$. Confluence orders $\{2, 2, 2\}$, root order
  $2$.

Both trees: four births at $\tau$, three deaths at the *same*
multiset $\{h_1, h_2, h_3\}$ (arrange branch cell-counts so the
confluence accumulations coincide), one infinite class. Barcode
$\{(\tau, h_1), (\tau, h_2), (\tau, h_3), (\tau, \infty)\}$ is
identical; Strahler distributions $\{2, 2, 3\}$ vs $\{2, 2, 2\}$
differ.

> **Why equal-birth, not "permute children."** With *distinct* birth
> heights the elder rule pairs each death with the younger merging
> component's birth, so permuting children changes the pairing and
> thus the barcode — bottleneck distance comes back nonzero and the
> witness fails. Equalizing births collapses the barcode to a
> death-multiset, which is the regime where the construction delivers
> identical barcodes by design. The Phase B test must *measure*
> $d_B \approx 0$ empirically, never assume the construction produced
> it.

*Proof obligation.* `[TO DRAFT — MK]` (a) Prove the abelianization
claim: $\operatorname{Dgm}_0$ retains birth/death heights and the
elder-rule pairing but forgets sibling order at each merge. (b) Verify
the two witness DEMs realize the claimed accumulation values so the
death multisets coincide exactly (this is the arithmetic step where a
stale mental model would slip in; check branch cell-counts against the
intended $h_1, h_2, h_3$). (c) Confirm Strahler distributions differ.
This matches the Phase B `test_falsification.py` fixture.

# 5. Theorem 3 (field-level stability, honestly scoped)

**Theorem 3.** For accumulation fields $A_1, A_2$ on a common mask,
$$
d_B\bigl(\operatorname{Dgm}_0(A_1), \operatorname{Dgm}_0(A_2)\bigr)
\;\le\; \lVert A_1 - A_2 \rVert_\infty .
$$
No global Lipschitz bound of the form
$\lVert A_1 - A_2 \rVert_\infty \le L \, \lVert z'_1 - z'_2 \rVert_\infty$
exists: D8 routing is discontinuous in elevation at saddles, so an
$\varepsilon$ perturbation can reroute an $O(\text{catchment size})$
mass of accumulation. This instability is a property of *drainage*
(small divide migrations genuinely reroute basins; a faithful drainage
metric reflects that), not a defect of the metric.

*Proof obligation.* `[TO DRAFT — MK]` (a) Apply the
Cohen-Steiner–Edelsbrunner–Harer (2007) bottleneck stability theorem
to $A$ as a function on the donor graph $\mathcal{G}$; confirm the
hypotheses transfer from the simplicial/cubical setting to this
graph filtration. (b) Give the saddle-flip counterexample explicitly:
two DEMs $\varepsilon$-close in $z'$ whose accumulation fields differ
by an entire catchment, defeating any finite $L$. (c) Optional:
state and sketch the generic-stability claim (DEMs off a measure-zero
set of saddle-tied configurations have $A$ stable under sufficiently
small perturbations).

# 6. Discussion sub-cases

`[TO DRAFT — MK]` each as a short paragraph; these neutralize the
predictable referee objections.

- **Flat regions.** The filtration operates on the breach-and-fill
  $z'$, where flats are resolved; the construction needs no separate
  flat-handling. State the dependence on the conditioning algorithm.
- **D8 vs D-infinity.** The bijection (Lemma) holds under D8 because
  single-receiver routing makes $\{x \to r(x)\}$ a forest, so the
  donor graph is a tree per basin and Strahler is well-defined. Under
  D-infinity (multiple receivers) the filtration is still defined but
  the coarsening map to Strahler is not a function. Mark D-infinity
  as future work.
- **Multi-outlet tiles.** A tile boundary cuts across divides, so the
  channel network is a *forest* of trees, one per basin reaching the
  boundary. The Lemma and Theorems 1-3 apply tree-by-tree; state this
  so the real-DEM construction in Phase C is covered.
- **Channel-mask choice.** $\tau_{\mathrm{chan}}$ is a modeling
  parameter set per-tile in Phase C to match NHD drainage density; no
  theorem depends on its value. The sweep over $\tau_{\mathrm{chan}}$
  recovers the full merge tree; a single threshold is one slice.

# 7. What flow-accumulation $H_0$ separates that elevation $H_0$ conflates {#sec-contrast}

> **Load-bearing section.** Per the folklore-collision profile, the
> elevation merge tree is thoroughly worked in prior art (TerraStream,
> Agarwal-Arge-Yi, Cousty et al.). A referee in that lineage will read
> the construction fluently and ask: *what does the flow-accumulation
> filtration buy over the elevation merge tree everyone already
> builds?* The novelty cannot rest on the adjacency choice alone
> (adjacency is a construction, not a result). It must land here. This
> section carries the paper's defense; budget it as a primary section,
> not a remark.

*Obligations.* `[TO DRAFT — MK]`

- **Divide-vs-channel asymmetry.** Show, with a worked example, a
  configuration where elevation-sublevel $H_0$ merges two components
  at a low saddle that flow accumulation keeps separate (or vice
  versa), so the elevation merge tree reports a basin relationship
  that the drainage does not have.
- **Basin hierarchy independent of absolute relief.** Show that two
  terrains with very different elevation ranges but the same drainage
  hierarchy have the same flow-accumulation merge tree while their
  elevation merge trees differ (or are incomparable). This is the
  "what flow accumulation buys" sentence in its strongest form.
- **Planned figure (the adjacency-choice demonstration).** The
  meander-neck mask, side by side: $H_1 = 8$ under cubical
  8-adjacency vs $H_1 = 0$ under donor-graph adjacency (Phase 0 spike;
  the count is hand-verified: 9 cells, 16 8-adjacency edges, one
  component, $H_1 = 16 - 9 + 1 = 8$). This converts "the adjacency
  choice is a real contribution" from an assertion into a one-glance
  demonstration, pre-empting the "adjacency is just an implementation
  detail" referee.

# Pre-submission checklist (carry forward)

- Deep read of Cousty-Najman, "Characterization of Graph-Based
  Hierarchical Watersheds," *JMIV* 2019 — the single paper most able
  to pre-empt the Lemma's framing (flagged in the folklore check;
  non-optional before submission).
- Confirm the Phase B `test_falsification.py` fixture realizes the
  §4 equal-birth construction with coinciding death multisets.
- Confirm the §7 elevation-contrast worked examples have matching
  Phase B toy fixtures so the prose claims are computationally backed.
